text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Discussing Solutions and Strategies

Have groups share how they connect the ratio to the diagrams to the percents.

Suggested Questions

  • How do you represent 38 out of 50 on a bar diagram?
  • How do you represent 38 out of 50 on a 100 grid?

• Some students might scale the ratio to 100 students first, then shade the bar. Others might say that every segment on the bar can represent 1/2 a student, so you shade in 2 partitions for each student.
  • Some students might want to use a grid of 50 rather than a grid of 100. Some might say that every student counts as 2 smaller squares, so you shade 2 for each student, or 76 squares. Others might change the ratio to 76 out of 100, then shade the grid.

Making the Mathematics Explicit

After relating the comparison statements to the percent representation, students will see that a part-to-whole comparison where the total is 100 converts directly to a percentage.

Suggested Questions

  • What would be a comparison statement and percent to describe What If...? Situation A Diagram A?
Various answers. An example might be "For every 100 little squares, there are 30 pink, or 30% of the squares are pink."
• What would be a comparison statement and percent to describe What If...? Situation A Diagram B?
Various answers. Students might say that for every 100 squares on the grid, 52 1/2 are pink, or 52.5% of the squares are pink.
• What would be a comparison statement and percent to describe What If...? Situation A Diagram C?

Students may say that for every 25 squares on the grid, there are 5 that are pink. They may scale both quantities by a multiple of 4 to get an equivalent ratio of 20 pink for every 100 total squares, or 20% of the grid is pink.
• What would be a comparison statement and percent to describe What If...? Situation A Diagram D?
Various answers. Students might say that for every 100 slices on the diagram, 75 are shaded pink, or 75% of the diagram is shaded pink.
• What would be a comparison statement and percent to describe What If...? Situation A Diagram E?

Various answers. This offers an opportunity for students to consider percentages over 100. Students may say that the top bar is completely shaded pink or 100% is pink. The second bar shows 20 pink slices for every 100 slices is shaded, or 20% is shaded pink. If we combine the percentages, 120% is shaded pink. Students might also sat that 120 slices are shaded pink for very 100 slices, or 120% is shaded pink.
• What type of ratio comparison statements help you to write percents?
Part-to-whole comparison where the whole is 100.

Uses students' examples of problems that they wrote to discuss using percents. Their problems will be a formative assessment for you to see what sense they are making of percents at this time. Also, you will learn what students know about applying percent ideas to solving problems. (Agency, Identity, Ownership)

Discuss the use of 100 strip as a percent bar in What If...? Situation B. teaching Aid 4.1: Keeya's tape Diagram might help the conversation if you reveal each step in Keeya's thinking and ask questions like the following.

Suggested Questions

  • If the whole bar represents 100%, what do the black partitions represent?
Partitions of the 100% in increments of 10. At the first ark, it would be 10%. THe next mark is 20%, then 30%, etc.
• What do the blue partitions represent?
Partitions of the 100% in increments of 1. At the first mark, it would be 1%. The next mark is 2%, then 3%, etc.
Reflecting on Student Learning

Now What Do Students Know

.
